title: Generic types and methods

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Generic types and methods; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Generics and Collections Framework learning paths; atomic topic: Generic types and methods
sources.claim: Java generic types and methods introduce compile-time type parameters so one implementation can preserve relationships among input, stored, and returned types without raw casts.

sources.locator: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21; Java SE 21; JLS §§4.5, 4.10, 5.1, and 8.4.4: parameterized types, subtyping, conversion, and generic methods; atomic topic: Generic types and methods
sources.claim: Java generic types and methods introduce compile-time type parameters so one implementation can preserve relationships among input, stored, and returned types without raw casts.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java generic types and methods introduce compile-time type parameters so one implementation can preserve relationships among input, stored, and returned types without raw casts.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Box<T> stores and returns T, while a static <T> T first(List<T>) method preserves the list element type at each call. Your goal is to implement and use a small generic class and generic method safely. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Interfaces; Arrays and multidimensional arrays; Method contracts and parameters. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java generic types and methods introduce compile-time type parameters so one implementation can preserve relationships among input, stored, and returned types without raw casts. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Type erasure restricts runtime type tests and generic array creation, primitives need wrappers, static members cannot use a class type parameter directly, and raw types discard checks. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Generic type arguments are fully reified at runtime, so new T() and new T[10] are always available.

includes: Parameterize reusable APIs and avoid raw types and unnecessary casts.
Implement and use a small generic class and generic method safely.
excludes: Type erasure restricts runtime type tests and generic array creation, primitives need wrappers, static members cannot use a class type parameter directly, and raw types discard checks.

outcomes: Implement and use a small generic class and generic method safely.

examples.title: Generic types and methods: direct language case
examples.situation: Box<T> stores and returns T, while a static <T> T first(List<T>) method preserves the list element type at each call.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Box<T> stores and returns T, while a static <T> T first(List<T>) method preserves the list element type at each call.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use type parameters for compile-time relationships and account for type erasure; avoid raw types and require factories or reifiable representations when runtime construction is needed.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use type parameters for compile-time relationships and account for type erasure; avoid raw types and require factories or reifiable representations when runtime construction is needed.

examples.title: Generic types and methods: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A raw List accepts a String into a collection expected to hold Integer, moving the failure to a later compiler-inserted cast.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A raw List accepts a String into a collection expected to hold Integer, moving the failure to a later compiler-inserted cast.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Type erasure restricts runtime type tests and generic array creation, primitives need wrappers, static members cannot use a class type parameter directly, and raw types discard checks.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Generic type arguments are fully reified at runtime, so new T() and new T[10] are always available.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement and use a small generic class and generic method safely.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Generic type arguments are fully reified at runtime, so new T() and new T[10] are always available.
misconceptions.correction: Use type parameters for compile-time relationships and account for type erasure; avoid raw types and require factories or reifiable representations when runtime construction is needed.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Generic type arguments are fully reified at runtime, so new T() and new T[10] are always available.

recap.summary: You should now be able to implement and use a small generic class and generic method safely. Keep this boundary in view: Type erasure restricts runtime type tests and generic array creation, primitives need wrappers, static members cannot use a class type parameter directly, and raw types discard checks.
